title: What is transaction?
summary: Understand autocommit, the risks of not using transactions, how to use SET AUTOCOMMIT=FALSE, START TRANSACTION, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK to ensure data security.
content_html:
What is transaction?

One transaction (transaction) is a unit of work consisting of one or more SQL commands executed as an atomic block — or all succeed, or all be undone.

Transaction guarantees properties ACID:

  • Atomicity: All or nothing
  • Consistency: Database is always in a valid state
  • Isolation: Transactions are independent of each other
  • Durability: Data after COMMIT exists forever

1. Autocommit — Risk of default

MySQL is enabled by default autocommit = ON. This means each DML statement (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) is automatically COMMITmed immediately — cannot be undone.

⚠️ Real story (Lecture Week 8)
A developer runs the command:
UPDATE Orders SET CustomerID = 999;
Forgot WHERE clause → 45,364 lines is updated incorrectly immediately and cannot be undone because autocommit is ON! 
SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'autocommit';

The result is usually ON by default.

2. Turn off Autocommit — SET AUTOCOMMIT=FALSE

Method 1: Turn off autocommit for the current session:

SET AUTOCOMMIT = FALSE;

UPDATE Orders SET CustomerID = 999 WHERE OrderID = 101;

SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE OrderID = 101;

ROLLBACK;

With AUTOCOMMIT=FALSE, the UPDATE command is not permanently saved. You can check the results, then decide to COMMIT or ROLLBACK.

Safe procedure with AUTOCOMMIT=FALSE

Workflow:
  1. SET AUTOCOMMIT = FALSE;
  2. Execute DML commands (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
  3. SELECT to check the results
  4. If true → COMMIT;
  5. If wrong → ROLLBACK;

3. START TRANSACTION — Clear transaction block

The best way to control transactions — use START TRANSACTION even when autocommit is ON:

START TRANSACTION;

INSERT INTO Account (AccID, Balance) VALUES (101, 1000);
INSERT INTO Account (AccID, Balance) VALUES (102, 500);

UPDATE Account SET Balance = Balance - 200 WHERE AccID = 101;
UPDATE Account SET Balance = Balance + 200 WHERE AccID = 102;

SELECT * FROM Account;

COMMIT;

The example above simulates a bank transfer: subtract 200 from account 101 and add 200 to account 102. Both must succeed, or both are undone.

4. ROLLBACK — Undo

ROLLBACK rolls back all changes since the start of the transaction (or since SET AUTOCOMMIT=FALSE):

START TRANSACTION;

DELETE FROM Staff WHERE BranchID = 101;

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Staff;

ROLLBACK;

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Staff;

After ROLLBACK, the line number in Staff returns to the same as before DELETE.

When to use ROLLBACK?

  • Detected errors in data after running DML commands
  • One of the transaction steps failed
  • Checked the data and found the results were not as expected

5. SAVEPOINT — Intermediate save point

START TRANSACTION;

INSERT INTO Staff (StaffID, StaffName) VALUES (10, 'Anna');
SAVEPOINT after_insert;

UPDATE Staff SET BranchID = 999 WHERE StaffID = 10;

ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT after_insert;

COMMIT;

Use SAVEPOINT to ROLLBACK to a point in the middle of the transaction, not to the beginning.

6. Locking and Isolation

When multiple transactions run in parallel, MySQL uses locks to avoid conflicts. The default isolation level is REPEATABLE READ.

Common problems with concurrent access:

  • Dirty Read: Read non-COMMIT data of another transaction
  • Non-repeatable Read: Read the same line twice but get different results
  • Phantom Read: Query returns the number of different lines in the same transaction

InnoDB (MySQL's default storage engine) fully supports ACID and row-level locking.
